title: Early Christianity: First Century to Constantine
subtitle: From the Crucifixion to the Council of Nicaea—Historical Events and Theological Emergence
summary: Early Christianity emerged from a Jewish apocalyptic movement following Jesus' crucifixion under Pontius Pilate (ca. 30 CE) into a distinct faith claiming his resurrection and redemptive significance. From Jerusalem to Rome, the movement expanded through persecution, the missionary work of Paul, and Constantine's legalization in 312 CE, culminating in the Council of Nicaea (325 CE) which established orthodox doctrine. Scholarly historians and faith traditions offer different emphases on causation, agency, and interpretation.

seo.metaTitle: Early Christianity: First Century to Constantine | The Straight Path
seo.metaDescription: Explore the historical emergence of early Christianity from the Crucifixion through Constantine's legalization and the Council of Nicaea, with Islamic and Christian perspectives.

sections.title: The Crucifixion and Death of Jesus
sections.body:
All four Gospels and Paul's letters attest to Jesus' death by crucifixion under Pontius Pilate, the Roman prefect of Judea, during the reign of Tiberius (14–37 CE). The event is dated approximately 30 CE, during Passover. Crucifixion was a brutal Roman execution method reserved for non-citizens and slaves, carrying profound shame in Jewish and Roman culture. The Gospels describe Jesus being arrested in Jerusalem, tried before the Jewish council (Sanhedrin), delivered to Pilate, and crucified at a location called Golgotha ("place of the skull"). According to Christian sources, Jesus died after a few hours on the cross.

From a scholarly historical perspective, the crucifixion is considered one of the most assured facts about Jesus' life, attested across independent sources and deemed deeply embarrassing to early Christian proclamation (a criterion scholars use for historicity). The humiliating nature of the death—inconsistent with messianic expectations—made it unlikely to be invented. Early Christians sought to interpret this apparent defeat through the lens of Scripture and theological meaning.

Islamic tradition, by contrast, affirms Jesus as a messenger and righteous prophet but disputes the crucifixion itself. The Quranic account presents the crucifixion as a false perception or substitution, stating that Jesus was not actually killed or crucified but was raised to God. Islamic scholars have developed various interpretations of this passage over centuries, but the consistent Islamic position holds that Jesus remains alive and will return at the end of time. This represents a fundamental theological divergence at the foundation of Christian-Islamic comparison.

sections.title: The Resurrection Claim and Earliest Testimony
sections.body:
The claim that Jesus rose from the dead became the central and transformative conviction of early Christianity. Scholarly historians treat the disciples' conviction that Jesus had been raised as the most probable explanation for Christianity's emergence; what precisely happened remains a matter of theological interpretation.

The earliest written formulation of the resurrection appears in Paul's first letter to Corinth, written circa 53–54 CE, less than twenty-five years after the crucifixion. Paul explicitly states he is transmitting a tradition he received, indicating this creedal formula predates his letter and likely originated in Jerusalem within just a few years of the events.

Paul writes: "For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. Then he appeared to over five hundred brothers at once, most of whom remain until now, but some have also fallen asleep. Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles, and last of all, as to the child born at the wrong time, he appeared to me also." This passage provides a structured list of resurrection appearances, establishing that multiple individuals and groups claimed to have encountered the risen Jesus.

The Gospels provide narrative accounts of the resurrection. Luke's Gospel states: "Now when the day of Pentecost had come, they were all with one accord in one place. Suddenly there came from the sky a sound like the rushing of a mighty wind, and it filled all the house where they were sitting." Acts also reports: "To these he also showed himself alive after he suffered, by many proofs, appearing to them over a period of forty days, and speaking about God’s Kingdom." These accounts emphasize both the physicality and the transformed nature of the risen body.

Faith traditions differ fundamentally in their interpretation: Christian theology holds the resurrection as literal, historical, and redemptively central—the reversal of death itself. Islamic theology maintains that God would not permit a messenger to be killed in that way, and interprets Quranic verses to mean Jesus was raised to God without undergoing crucifixion and death. Both traditions appeal to Scripture as authority but read the same historical events through different theological frameworks.

sections.title: Formation of the Jerusalem Church
sections.body:
Following the crucifixion, the disciples regrouped in Jerusalem despite the risk of arrest. Acts describes the apostles—Peter, John, and others—continuing Jesus' teaching, performing healings, and gathering followers. Peter emerges as the primary leader, though James, Jesus' brother, later takes a leading role in the Jerusalem community. Acts 2:22-24 records Peter's early proclamation: "“Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know, him, being delivered up by the determined counsel and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by the hand of lawless men, crucified and killed; whom God raised up, having freed him from the agony of death, because it was not possible that he should be held by it."

The Jerusalem church maintained Jewish identity and practice—observing the Sabbath, attending the Temple, following dietary laws. Acts describes a community marked by shared possessions, frequent meals together, and adherence to Torah. The apostles were initially perceived as a renewal movement within Judaism rather than a separate religion. Persecution from Jewish authorities (recorded in Acts and evidenced in Paul's own letters) escalated tensions but did not eliminate the movement.

The theological center of the Jerusalem church was the conviction that Jesus was the Messiah (christos in Greek, mashiach in Hebrew) and that his death and resurrection fulfilled scriptural prophecies. However, fundamental questions emerged: Was Jesus divine? How should gentiles be incorporated if they did not follow Jewish law? These tensions would shape the church's development.

sections.title: Paul and the Expansion Beyond Judaism
sections.body:
A pivotal figure in early Christianity's transformation was Saul of Tarsus, later known as Paul. A Hellenistic Jew from Asia Minor, Paul initially persecuted Christians on behalf of Jewish authorities. According to Acts and Paul's own letters, he underwent a dramatic conversion after claiming a vision of the risen Jesus. Paul's conversion marked a theological turning point: he believed that faith in Jesus' resurrection and redemptive death superseded the requirement to observe Jewish law, opening Christianity to non-Jewish followers.

Romans 1:1-4 presents Paul's foundational theology: "Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, set apart for the Good News of God, which he promised before through his prophets in the holy Scriptures, concerning his Son, who was born of the offspring of David according to the flesh, who was declared to be the Son of God with power, according to the Spirit of holiness, by the resurrection from the dead, Jesus Christ our Lord." This articulates the claim that Jesus was declared Son of God through the resurrection.

Paul's missionary journeys, documented in Acts and referenced in his letters, established communities throughout the eastern Mediterranean: Antioch, Corinth, Ephesus, Philippi, Thessalonica, and Rome. His letters to these communities, written circa 50–64 CE, constitute the earliest Christian writings that survive. They address practical problems (divisions, sexual ethics, relationship to Jewish law), theological questions (Christ's redemptive role, resurrection of believers), and ecclesial organization.

Crucially, Paul's theology shifted the interpretive center from Jewish expectation of a political messiah to a cosmic redemption narrative: Jesus' death as sacrifice for sins, his resurrection as the firstfruits of believers' own resurrection, and his parousia (return) as the culmination of history. This universalizing theology facilitated Christianity's spread beyond Jewish communities but also generated conflict with Jerusalem authorities who saw Paul as compromising Torah observance.

sections.title: The Role of Pentecost and Early Communion
sections.body:
Christian tradition marks Pentecost—the Jewish harvest festival fifty days after Passover—as the moment the Holy Spirit empowered the apostles with boldness to proclaim the resurrection. Acts 2:1-4 describes the event: "Now when the day of Pentecost had come, they were all with one accord in one place. Suddenly there came from the sky a sound like the rushing of a mighty wind, and it filled all the house where they were sitting. Tongues like fire appeared and were distributed to them, and one sat on each of them. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, and began to speak with other languages, as the Spirit gave them the ability to speak."

This narrative establishes several themes central to early Christian identity: the Holy Spirit as an active, empowering presence; the ability to communicate across linguistic boundaries (prefiguring the universal mission); and the experience of divine presence as authenticating the resurrection claim. The earliest church gathered for prayer, instruction, and the "breaking of bread" (communion/eucharist), understood as commemoration and participation in Christ's redemptive death and resurrection.

Matthew 28:18-20 records Jesus' final command to his followers: "Jesus came to them and spoke to them, saying, “All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth. Go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all things that I commanded you. Behold, I am with you always, even to the end of the age.” Amen." This commission established the church's mandate for worldwide mission and the theological authority of the risen Jesus.

sections.title: Persecution and Spread
sections.body:
Early Christians faced opposition from both Jewish and Roman authorities. Acts records the arrests and trials of Peter, John, Stephen (the first martyr), and Paul. Jewish leaders saw the new movement as a threat to communal cohesion and Temple authority. By the end of the first century, Christians were formally separated from synagogal fellowship.

Roman persecution emerged from different causes. Initially, Romans viewed Christianity as a variant of Judaism and granted it some tolerance under Roman law's protection of ancient religions. However, as Christianity became visibly distinct and rejected civic participation (particularly worship of the emperor and state gods), imperial suspicion grew. The Roman historian Pliny the Younger, writing to Emperor Trajan in the early second century, describes Christians gathering before dawn to sing hymns, share meals, and make covenants against murder and theft. He reports they refused to worship the emperor's statue.

The Neronian persecution (64 CE), recorded by the historians Suetonius and Tacitus, targeted Christians in Rome after a massive fire. Though Paul and Peter may have died under Nero, the scale of this persecution was likely limited to Rome itself. More systematic persecution came intermittently throughout the second and third centuries under emperors like Hadrian, Marcus Aurelius, Decius, and Diocletian.

Paradoxically, persecution did not eliminate Christianity but accelerated its spread. Tertullian, a Christian apologist writing around 200 CE, observed that "the blood of Christians is seed"—implying that martyrdom attracted new converts and deepened commitment. Christian texts valorizing martyrdom (Acts of the Martyrs) circulated widely, creating a theology of redemptive suffering that gave meaning to persecution. By the third century, Christianity had become sufficiently widespread and organized that emperors recognized it as a force requiring either suppression or accommodation.

sections.title: Constantine's Legalization and the Council of Nicaea
sections.body:
The trajectory of early Christianity shifted decisively in 312 CE when Constantine, a Roman general competing for sole imperial power, defeated a rival at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge outside Rome. Constantine credited his victory to the God of the Christians and subsequently issued the Edict of Milan (313 CE), which granted Christians legal status and freedom from persecution. Constantine's conversion—whether it was sincere faith, political calculation, or both—transformed Christianity from a persecuted minority into an imperial religion.

Constantine moved his capital from Rome to Byzantium (renamed Constantinople), which he intended as a Christian city. He funded church buildings, exempted clergy from taxation, and began organizing Christianity under imperial oversight. This legalization ended martyrdom as a defining Christian experience and raised new challenges: How should a religion shaped by countercultural opposition integrate with state power? How should doctrine be standardized across scattered communities with varying practices?

These questions came to a head in disputes over Christology—the nature of Christ. The Egyptian presbyter Arius taught that the Son was created by the Father and therefore subordinate and distinct in substance. Arius' teaching threatened the emergent consensus that Christ was divine and equal to the Father. In 325 CE, Constantine convened the First Ecumenical Council at Nicaea (in modern-day Turkey) to resolve the dispute.

The Council of Nicaea, attended by approximately 300 bishops, formulated the Nicene Creed, which declared that the Son was "of one substance" (homoousios in Greek) with the Father—affirming both Christ's full divinity and essential equality with God. Arius and his followers were condemned as heretical and exiled. The council also standardized Easter calculations, established metropolitan church structure, and asserted the authority of ecumenical councils to define orthodoxy.

From an Islamic perspective, this council and its theological decisions represent a fundamental development away from monotheism (tawhid) toward trinitarianism, which Islamic theology views as a corruption of Jesus' original monotheistic message. Islamic sources, including the Quran, emphasize Jesus as a human messenger calling people to worship God alone, without partners. The Quranic verse 5:117 records Jesus saying: "I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." This reflects the Islamic understanding that Jesus taught strict monotheism, not his own divinity.

The Nicene Council thus represents from a Christian perspective the clarification and defense of apostolic faith against heretical distortion, while from an Islamic perspective it marks a decisive turn toward theological positions incompatible with prophetic monotheism. Both traditions can acknowledge that this council was historically consequential and theologically momentous; they differ fundamentally in evaluating its rightness.

sections.title: Historical Sources and Faith Interpretations
sections.body:
Historical knowledge of early Christianity derives from multiple sources. The New Testament texts (Gospels, Paul's letters, Acts, and other epistles) were written within the first century and represent the earliest Christian literature, though they were composed after the events they describe and selected as authoritative through a process extending into the fourth century. These texts are primary sources for Christian belief and practice but reflect the perspectives of their authors and communities.

Extra-biblical sources include writings by the early Church Fathers (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus, Tertullian, Origen), who defended Christian teachings and documented church practices. Pagan writers (Pliny, Tacitus, Celsus) mention Christians and describe their practices, providing external perspectives. Archaeological evidence (inscriptions, burial sites, church structures) offers material corroboration.

Historians approach these sources with critical methods: comparing accounts for consistency, considering authors' biases and communities, analyzing linguistic and textual development, and assessing plausibility against broader historical knowledge. From this scholarly perspective, certain events have high probability (Jesus' crucifixion, the disciples' conviction they encountered the risen Jesus, the movement's rapid expansion, persecution, Constantine's legalization), while other matters (exact resurrection appearances, Paul's exact conversion experience) remain more contested.

Faith interpretations, by contrast, read the scriptural accounts as divinely inspired testimony. Christian theology holds that the resurrection was a real historical event (not merely a spiritual or psychological experience) that vindicated Jesus and proved his redemptive significance. Islamic theology reads the Quranic accounts as God's correction of distorted reports. Both traditions treat their scriptures as authoritative, but they differ in which texts receive priority and how they interpret overlapping narratives.

Scholarly historical inquiry and faith-based interpretation operate on different epistemic foundations: history seeks naturalistic explanation and probability; faith embraces divine agency and revelation. Both approaches can be intellectually rigorous within their frameworks. Confusion arises when the two are conflated or when one claims the authority proper to the other.

sections.title: Core Theological Divergences
sections.body:
Early Christianity developed theological claims about Jesus that diverged fundamentally from Jewish monotheism and eventually from Islamic theology. The central claims were: Jesus was the Messiah (the anointed one expected to restore Israel); Jesus was the Son of God (understood increasingly as divine, not merely as a title of honor); Jesus' death was a sacrifice for humanity's sins; Jesus' resurrection vindicated his claims and assured believers of their own future resurrection; Jesus would return to judge the living and the dead; through Jesus, God became incarnate in human form.

These claims represented a radical innovation in monotheistic thinking. Jewish Christianity (and modern Jewish thought) rejected Jesus' messianic and divine status while accepting him as a righteous teacher. Islamic theology accepts Jesus as a supremely honored prophet and messiah but emphatically rejects his divinity, his crucifixion, and the notion that God became human. The Islamic position, articulated in the Quran, is that God is utterly transcendent and has no partners; to associate partners with God (shirk) is the gravest sin. Surah 4:157 states: "And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain." This presents the Islamic correction of the crucifixion claim.

Christian theologians from the Church Fathers onward developed sophisticated philosophical arguments for the incarnation and the Trinity, using Greco-Roman philosophical categories. Defenders of Nicene orthodoxy argued that the Trinity preserved monotheism ("one God in three persons") and that the incarnation demonstrated God's redemptive love. Critics, including Arius and later Arianism, saw the Trinity as compromising monotheism and argued for Christ's subordination to the Father.

Islamic theology maintains that both Trinitarian Christianity and incarnational theology represent deviations from the pure monotheism taught by all prophets, including Jesus himself. Surah 2:87 affirms: "And We did certainly give Moses the Torah and followed up after him with messengers. And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit. But is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you, [O Children of Israel], with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant? And a party [of messengers] you denied and another party you killed..." This positions Jesus as a messenger in a continuous line of prophets, not as God incarnate.

From a comparative perspective, both traditions developed sophisticated theological frameworks addressing similar existential questions: How does God relate to the world and humanity? What role does the ultimate messenger/messiah play in human salvation and divine purpose? What constitutes authentic monotheism? The answers diverged radically, but the questions themselves reveal a common religious project: integrating trust in God's oneness with historical revelation.

sections.title: Institutional Development and Organizational Structure
sections.body:
As Christianity expanded geographically and numerically, it developed increasingly formal organizational structures. In the first century, churches were led by apostles, prophets, and teachers, often collectively. By the second century, a threefold ministry emerged: bishops (episkopos), presbyters (elders), and deacons. The bishop eventually became the chief executive of a local church, responsible for teaching, discipline, and liturgy.

This hierarchical structure served multiple functions: it centralized authority in the face of false teaching (what leaders called "heresy"), it provided continuity and stability as first-generation apostles died, it facilitated communication across geographically scattered communities, and it formalized the process of interpreting Scripture and settling disputes. By the second century, bishops and elders claimed authority based on apostolic succession—the claim that they stood in an unbroken chain of authorization extending back to the apostles themselves.

The Canon (the collection of authoritative texts) developed gradually across the second through fourth centuries. Different regions initially recognized different texts; eventually, the New Testament canon was largely settled by consensus, though disputes persisted into later centuries. This process of canonization established Scripture's authority and constrained interpretative diversity.

The Creeds (Nicene, later expanded at Constantinople and Chalcedon) functioned as interpretive summaries of Scripture, defining the boundaries of orthodoxy. Creeds provided concise formulations for baptism, catechesis (instruction of new believers), and doctrinal controversy. They represented an attempt to standardize Christian belief across diverse communities and to exclude teachings deemed false.

Worship practices also became increasingly formalized. Baptism emerged as the initiatory rite, performed in the name of the Trinity (as commanded in Matthew 28:18-20). The Eucharist (Lord's Supper, Communion) was celebrated weekly or regularly, understood variously as memorial, sacrament, or mystery. Prayers, readings from Scripture, and hymn-singing structured corporate worship. By the fourth century, the basic structure of the Christian liturgy was largely established.

This institutionalization represented both gain and loss from different perspectives. Gain: standardization, clarity, stability, and ability to transmit tradition across generations and regions. Loss: spontaneity, prophetic critique, flexibility, and the sense of eschatological urgency that characterized earliest Christianity. Constantine's legalization accelerated this process, as the church now had imperial resources and oversight, transforming it from a persecuted sect into a imperial institution.

quranVerses.arabic: وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّۢ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا
quranVerses.translation: And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ
quranVerses.translation: [Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ شَهِيدٌ
quranVerses.translation: I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًۭا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًۭا تَقْتُلُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And We did certainly give Moses the Torah and followed up after him with messengers. And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit. But is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you, [O Children of Israel], with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant? And a party [of messengers] you denied and another party you killed.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. Then he appeared to over five hundred brothers at once, most of whom remain until now, but some have also fallen asleep. Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles, and last of all, as to the child born at the wrong time, he appeared to me also.

“Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know, him, being delivered up by the determined counsel and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by the hand of lawless men, crucified and killed; whom God raised up, having freed him from the agony of death, because it was not possible that he should be held by it.

But on the first day of the week, at early dawn, they and some others came to the tomb, bringing the spices which they had prepared. They found the stone rolled away from the tomb. They entered in, and didn’t find the Lord Jesus’ body. While they were greatly perplexed about this, behold, two men stood by them in dazzling clothing. Becoming terrified, they bowed their faces down to the earth. They said to them, “Why do you seek the living among the dead? He isn’t here, but is risen. Remember what he told you when he was still in Galilee,
To these he also showed himself alive after he suffered, by many proofs, appearing to them over a period of forty days, and speaking about God’s Kingdom.

Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, set apart for the Good News of God, which he promised before through his prophets in the holy Scriptures, concerning his Son, who was born of the offspring of David according to the flesh, who was declared to be the Son of God with power, according to the Spirit of holiness, by the resurrection from the dead, Jesus Christ our Lord,

Now when the day of Pentecost had come, they were all with one accord in one place. Suddenly there came from the sky a sound like the rushing of a mighty wind, and it filled all the house where they were sitting. Tongues like fire appeared and were distributed to them, and one sat on each of them. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, and began to speak with other languages, as the Spirit gave them the ability to speak.

Jesus came to them and spoke to them, saying, “All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth. Go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all things that I commanded you. Behold, I am with you always, even to the end of the age.” Amen.

faq.question: What is the historical evidence for Jesus' crucifixion?
faq.answer: The crucifixion is attested in all four Gospels, in Paul's letters (1 Corinthians 15:3-8), and in non-Christian sources including the Roman historian Tacitus and the Jewish historian Josephus. Historians consider it one of the most assured facts about Jesus because: (1) it appears in independent sources, (2) it would have been embarrassing to Christian preaching (suggesting a failed messiah), and (3) crucifixion by Rome was a recognized Roman execution method. This near-unanimous conclusion of mainstream academic historical-critical scholarship—including scholars from a range of religious backgrounds—is a historical judgment about what most likely occurred, distinct from normative Islamic theology, which denies the event itself took place. The Quran (4:157) states that Jesus was 'not killed... nor crucified' but that it was made to appear so. This is therefore a genuine disagreement about what happened, not merely a difference in how a mutually-agreed event is interpreted.

faq.question: What did early Christians believe about Jesus' resurrection, and how do we know?
faq.answer: The earliest written testimony appears in Paul's first letter to Corinth (1 Corinthians 15:3-8), written about 20-25 years after the crucifixion. Paul states he is passing on a tradition received from earlier Christians in Jerusalem, indicating the belief predates his letter. The formula mentions multiple appearance accounts to different individuals and groups. Scholars debate what actually happened (literal bodily resurrection, visionary experiences, spiritual transformation, etc.), but historical consensus is strong that the disciples were convinced Jesus had been raised and that this conviction drove the emergence of Christianity. Islamic theology, by contrast, affirms Jesus' elevation to God but denies crucifixion and literal bodily death, making resurrection unnecessary within Islamic Christology.

faq.question: How did Christianity spread so rapidly despite persecution?
faq.answer: Several factors accelerated Christianity's expansion: (1) Paul's missionary strategy of establishing churches in major urban centers across the Mediterranean, (2) the movement's appeal to socially marginalized groups (women, slaves, poor) who found dignity and community, (3) the monotheistic and salvific message resonated with those dissatisfied with pagan religions, (4) the organizational structure (bishops, presbyters, deacons) provided stability and coherence, (5) the martyrdom narratives valorized suffering and attracted committed converts, and (6) Christianity's flexibility in adapting to local cultures while maintaining core beliefs. By the third century, Christianity had become so widespread that emperors could no longer ignore it.

faq.question: What was the significance of Constantine's conversion for Christianity?
faq.answer: Constantine's conversion in 312 CE and his subsequent legalization of Christianity through the Edict of Milan (313 CE) transformed the religion from a persecuted minority into an imperial religion. This legalization ended persecution, allowed Christian institutional growth, and eventually made Christianity the dominant religion of the empire. However, it also raised new challenges: integrating a countercultural faith with state power, standardizing doctrine across diverse communities, and addressing the tension between spiritual calling and political establishment. The Council of Nicaea (325 CE) represents Constantine's attempt to use imperial authority to enforce doctrinal uniformity.

faq.question: Why did the Council of Nicaea matter, and what did it decide?
faq.answer: The Council of Nicaea (325 CE) was convened by Constantine to resolve the Arian controversy—a dispute over whether Christ was divine and equal to the Father, or created and subordinate. The council affirmed the Nicene Creed, which declared that the Son was 'of one substance' (homoousios) with the Father, thereby establishing Christ's full divinity and equality with God. The council also standardized Easter calculations and established ecclesiastical structure. From a Christian perspective, Nicaea defended apostolic faith against heresy. From an Islamic perspective, it represents a decisive move toward Trinitarianism, which Islamic theology views as a corruption of Jesus' original monotheistic message calling for worship of God alone.

faq.question: How do Christian and Islamic interpretations of early Christianity differ?
faq.answer: Christian and Islamic traditions agree on many historical facts (Jesus' ministry, crucifixion under Pilate, the disciples' conviction of resurrection, early persecution, Constantine's legalization) but diverge fundamentally in theological interpretation. Christianity affirms Jesus as divine Son of God, whose death was a redemptive sacrifice, whose resurrection vindicated his divinity, and whose return is anticipated. Islam affirms Jesus as a supremely honored prophet and messiah but emphatically rejects his divinity, his crucifixion (Quran 4:157), and incarnation. Islamic theology maintains that Jesus taught strict monotheism (Quran 5:117) and that later Christians departed from his original message. Both traditions see their interpretation as faithful to Scripture; they differ in which texts receive priority and how they read overlapping narratives.

furtherReading.title: The Historical Jesus: A Comprehensive Guide
furtherReading.author: Gerd Theissen and Annette Merz
furtherReading.note: A comprehensive scholarly overview of Jesus and early Christianity that integrates historical-critical methods with systematic theological reflection. Fortress Press, 1998. Accessible while maintaining academic rigor.

furtherReading.title: The Apostolic Fathers: Greek Texts and English Translations
furtherReading.author: Michael W. Holmes (translator and editor)
furtherReading.note: Authoritative bilingual edition of the Apostolic Fathers (Clement, Ignatius, Polycarp, Didache, and others) with Greek texts and English translations. Baker Academic, 3rd ed., 2007. Primary sources that illuminate early church life, theology, and responses to persecution.

furtherReading.title: The Cambridge Ancient History Vol. XI: The High Empire, A.D. 70-192
furtherReading.author: Edited by Alan K. Bowman, Peter Garnsey, Dominic Rathbone
furtherReading.note: Includes chapters on early Christianity within its broader Roman historical context, balancing Christian sources with external historical evidence.

furtherReading.title: Jesus and the Victory of God
furtherReading.author: N.T. Wright
furtherReading.note: A detailed scholarly work integrating Jesus within first-century Jewish expectations and showing how the resurrection transformed those expectations. Represents informed Christian scholarly perspective.
